label: Strong Performance
transcript:
Supervisor: Thanks for meeting with me. I want to start by recognizing how strong your performance was this month.
Employee: Thank you. I worked hard to stay ahead of the priorities.
Supervisor: It showed. You closed every assigned task before the deadline, your client updates were clear, and your documentation was some of the best on the team.
Employee: I tried to be more disciplined about communication and end-of-day notes.
Supervisor: That effort made a real difference. I also noticed you stepped in to help the new analyst without letting your own work slip.
Employee: I was glad to help. I remember how overwhelming the first few weeks can feel.
Supervisor: That kind of leadership matters. If I had one stretch goal for you, it would be to take more ownership in meetings and speak up with recommendations earlier.
Employee: That is fair. I can prepare talking points ahead of time so I contribute more confidently.
Supervisor: Great plan. Overall, this was a very strong month with consistent execution, dependable communication, and good judgment.

label: Average Performance
transcript:
Supervisor: I wanted to check in on how things have been going over the past two weeks.
Employee: I think I have been keeping up, but I know there is still room to improve.
Supervisor: That is a fair assessment. Your work has been reliable overall, and you are usually responsive when something urgent comes in.
Employee: I am glad to hear that.
Supervisor: At the same time, your updates can be inconsistent. Some days I get a clear status, and other days I have to follow up to understand where things stand.
Employee: Yes, I know I have been uneven there, especially when I am juggling multiple requests.
Supervisor: The quality of the work itself is acceptable, but I want to see more consistency in communication and a bit more attention to detail before handoff.
Employee: Understood. I can start sending a short daily summary and review deliverables one more time before I submit them.
Supervisor: That would help. You are in a solid middle ground right now, but stronger follow-through and clearer communication would move you up quickly.

label: Poor Communication / Performance
transcript:
Supervisor: We need to have a direct conversation about your recent performance and communication.
Employee: Okay.
Supervisor: Over the last three weeks, several deadlines were missed, and in multiple cases I did not hear about the delays until after the work was already late.
Employee: I know things slipped. I was trying to catch up before saying anything.
Supervisor: That approach created more risk for the team. When communication stops, we cannot help, reassign work, or reset expectations with clients.
Employee: I understand.
Supervisor: I also reviewed the last two deliverables, and both had avoidable errors that should have been caught before submission.
Employee: I was rushing and did not do a final review.
Supervisor: That is part of the problem. Right now, the issues are not just quality, they are reliability, visibility, and follow-through.
Employee: What would you like me to change first?
Supervisor: Starting immediately, I expect a daily status update, early escalation when you are blocked, and a checklist review before anything is submitted. We need to see clear improvement very quickly.
